claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm asking about my son Alex, who's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I can see you have a son named Alex who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is over 16, his eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether he's in approved education or training. 

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (This includes things like A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs, or other approved courses.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he's just left school recently, but he's signed up with the careers service to figure out what he wants to do next. He's in that sort of in-between stage at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. Alex has left education and registered with a careers service, which is relevant to your eligibility.

According to the rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for **20 weeks** if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services.

To assess this properly, I need to know: **How long ago did Alex leave school and register with the careers service?** (Is it within the last 20 weeks, or has it been longer?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been within the last 20 weeks, yes. He registered not long after leaving school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. And just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a couple more questions:

**Does Alex live with you, or are you contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards his upkeep** (such as for food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just one final question to complete the assessment:

**Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he receiving any benefits in his own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't started any paid work like that, and he's not receiving any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided:

✓ Alex is 17 years old and has left school  
✓ He registered with a careers service within the last 20 weeks  
✓ You live in the UK (Manchester)  
✓ Alex lives with you  
✓ He's not working 24+ hours per week or claiming benefits in his own right

**You can continue to claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks** from when Alex registered with the careers service. This gives him time to figure out his next steps while you continue to receive support.

**Important to note:** This 20-week eligibility period applies while Alex is registered with the careers service and looking for his next opportunity. If he starts approved education or training during this time, your eligibility could continue beyond the 20 weeks. However, if he starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week, your Child Benefit would stop.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has recently left school. He has registered with a government-sponsored careers service within the last 20 weeks. According to the Child Benefit rules, you can claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), Alex lives with them (establishing responsibility for the child), and Alex has not started paid work for 24 hours or more per week, nor is he receiving benefits in his own right. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex during this 20-week period following his registration with the careers service.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex. Although Alex has left school, he registered with a careers service within the last 20 weeks, which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks during this transitional period. The claimant meets all other eligibility criteria: they live in the UK, Alex lives with them, and Alex is not working 24+ hours per week or claiming benefits in his own right.

According to the Child Benefit rules, you can claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), Alex lives with them (establishing responsibility for the child), and Alex has not started paid work for 24 hours or more per week, nor is he receiving benefits in his own right. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex during this 20-week period following his registration with the careers service.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex. Although Alex has left school, he registered with a careers service within the last 20 weeks, which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks during this transitional period. The claimant meets all other eligibility criteria: they live in the UK, Alex lives with them, and Alex is not working 24+ hours per week or claiming benefits in his own right.
